channel.title: A new contest!
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "A new contest!"

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: May have a new angle with SCP-4023-EX. Hope it's not too technical to be fun.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radioluminescence

I guess radium's decay causes excitation of its own electrons, or those of other compounds mixed with it, which then emit light.

EDIT: Now I'm confused, as that seems to imply radium also needs to be mixed with a phosphor agent. Might glow without a phosphor by excitation of its own electrons, but the addition of the phosphor strengthens the glow.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: SCP-9912-EX crosses criticality.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: You know, I had an idea a while back for how some kinds of SCPs could be "naturalized" so that they would no longer be considered supernatural in character, even if they were a bit odd. Maybe now is a good time to implement that.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: Actually it's quite similiar to one of the anomalous items we have on the site…but Bright likes it, so it should work well as an -EX too.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: I doubt the Foundation is that old, if you ask me I would put it somewhere near the Age of Enlightment when science began to take hold (that would be late 18th century if I remember correctly). Otherwise I would see no reason for a Foundation to be formed, unless it's something like the Pope commissioning a guild of professional theologists and philosophers to prevent harmful and blasphemous material from getting exposed to the general God-fearing Christian public.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: Might work.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
There's a huge difference between: "Huh, this is interesting, I'm not sure why this happens. I will investigate further and form a theory once I know more." and "Ghosts! Ghosts all up in this!"

The former is productive, the latter is not.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
So much dammit, Dr. Bright.

On a more serious note, if the SCP was one of the (if there were multiple) purported lodestone mountains that would sink ships by tearing the nails out of any ship that strayed too near, made -EX once magnets were understood, I could see that working. Maybe even working well. Until it's understood, it's a spiteful sentinel with a watchful eye on the sea surrounding that flays ships as a fisherman guts a fish, but you would have to set this many centuries ago to work.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me of a story I was working on where a book the makes people who read it go insane turns out to just be covered in contact poison.

I've been meaning to ask about a poison for that, and say that it's for a book.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
assume that everything not currently understood was in some way paranormal

If that's not what paranormal means, what is? If it's something like that it can't be understood by science, what has the Foundation been doing?

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: I was thinking of a complete copy of the Encyclopedia Britannica from 2009, that fell through a temporal rift into 1968. Now, of course, it's 2010, and any events are either in the past or were averted and became part of another timeline, and the temporal rift never recurred, so it's now -EX. Whaddaya think?

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: Maybe it will help if I state that the first SCP idea I had for this was Magnets. Metallic stones that have the ability to move metal without touching it. Until you figure out a reason FOR them to work, they seem like, well, a miracle *don't stone me*

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: Ah, my bad. I was never any good at Chemistry…

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: Maybe it's too far along the "oh look this real thing was originally assumed to be an SCP" kind of angle, but some of the things so-called extremophile bacteria manage to do is pretty interesting. Eating acidic toxins deep in the pitch-darkness of oceanic chasms and violating a lot of biological expectations, and suchlike.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
Agreed. Man I am still at a loss for what to do. The best angle I can think of is that the Foundation had, at one point, pretty much contained one of our horrible real world epidemics and then later released it after study confirmed that it was a new but "mundane" disease. And putting the Foundation's fingerprints on something like that for the chills seems cheesy.

I'll think of something.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Demon_core

To my knowledge it was the only core to go critical and claim lives in that fashion during those early tests

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: I don't think the Foundation is that dumb.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: Demon Core?

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
The Demon Core only worked as an SCP in the sense that it was willfully malevolent, a bomb that was cheated of its destiny to explode. There's no way to include that angle and then explain it short of having the SCP royally fuck up and mistake the core as sapient but whoops! It was just radioactive.

Maybe it could work though. I do miss the Demon Core.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
Apparently, yes it does. I can't find any explanation of the mechanic behind this and would be very interested to find it.

Whatever the case, to the best of my knowledge, no naturally occuring radioactive ore glows. Normally radioluminescence requires the addition of a phosphor or similar agent that is excited by the radiation and later drops down to a lower energy state, releasing visible light in the process. Radium appears to be the exception, but I suspect its luminescence is due to a chemical process (possibly part of the oxidation?), not a radioactive one: radioactive decay just isn't visible.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: Maybe this is the venue for the Demon Core.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I know what you mean. My original idea for a sacrificial relic called for the reveal that the helmet wasn't "magical", it was just that an insane rich person was hiring people to kill over it… for kicks. That was too far fetched, so re-tweaked it… and now its un-EXy and more like a Safe SCP. Maybe I'm trying too hard to echo yoric's colour virus, and should just try something with a broader concept.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: Doesn't Radium glow?

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. I'm finding it really hard to write anything EX without it coming out with a strong "clever dick" flavour. By nature it seems to demand that something considered mundane or at least physically normal is in fact of a unique or supernatural origin. That doesn't make for very good articles really.

That said, someone may soon twist it into something truly interesting, so don't take my criticism too seriously. I'm still working on ideas, too, maybe something will come out of the murk that shines a bit of light onto what sort of material could be cool here.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: Pitchblende doesn't glow. Nothing radioactive glows. Further, it isn't all that radioactive unless the uranium is purified and enriched, certainly not enough that anyone ever made a connection between pitchblende and mysterious deaths :)

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
Glowy rocks? Uranium ore? Magically killing everyone within a certain radius after longterm exposure? SCP-XXX-EX: Death Rock?

Or at least that was what I first thought when I saw the word glowy…

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: Of course. All I'm saying is to make no assumptions without supporting evidence and keep things in perspective.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
True, but it'd all depend on how and when the rocks were discovered.

It's just an example really, not any kind of defining guide.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
Because at first they would think the glowing rocks were supernatural.

That's precisely what I'm saying is wrong with the scenario - that they just assume it's supernatural because it does something a little odd as opposed to first ruling out rational explanations. The Foundation is composed of scientists, not tinfoil hatters who want to believe. Granted, there would surely be a few among their ranks, but they would be in the minority.

Another issue with the scenario is that there would then be no unexplained curiosities nor phenomena because by the time anyone would hear of them, the Foundation would have already explained them, not to mention the enormous drain on resources that investigating every peculiarity in existence would undoubtedly entail, but I digress.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
Because at first they would think the glowing rocks were supernatural.
Thus, when they find out the rocks aren't they can desginate them whatever -EX and sell them as the Sedimentary Candle People.

Whereas the possibility nuclear weapons was scientifically investigated and designed from the ground up and clearly had no supernatural properties, regardless of how horrific they are.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
Why would they suppress the knowledge of how to make a rock glow and not, say, the construction of nuclear weapons? One of these things is a much greater threat to normalcy than the other, and neither are in any way supernatural phenomena.

If anything, I think it would serve to make the Foundation appear idiotic should they assume that everything not currently understood was in some way paranormal or endeavor to suppress knowledge "just because." The Foundation, in my mind, gets involved after all rational explanation has been ruled out, and not before such has even been attempted.

I would expect the Foundation to thoroughly investigate said glowing rock and then patent LED technology to supplement existing sources of funding, but not suppress knowledge of it at any point.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
I think it would be cool to do something like Herbertsmithite. It's believed to be a String-net liquid. They were first hypothesised in 1983, and the Foundation is older than that, so it's possible they found out about it before anybody knew what it was.

Of course, it doesn't seem to have any really weird effects.

There's probably several other things like that. I've read about something where someone found out that if you put a current through a certain rock, it glows. It was a really weak LED, but this was before the physics of them were understood.

What would be best would be something that was found shortly after the physics required to understand it were discovered, as this is what it would look like if the Foundation was suppressing it until then.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: A neutralized is one we've gotten rid of. For example: If we killed 682, it would be -N. If we turned 682 into some kind of every day, ordinary creature, that people would be used to seeing, and not think twice about, it would be -EX.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: A little goofier than I was looking for, but yes.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: What is the distinction between a neutralized SCP and an SCP-EX? Because for both, you can fit the definition of "complete non issue."

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: SCP-8900-EX is up.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: Coelacanth is a great example of an -EX.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
That would be -N for Neutralized, actually.

I'm thinking Coelacanth being an example?

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: Would a deceased living SCP count?

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: No, the above example would still be considered safe, or anomalous item.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: We'll see. A lot of it depends on how many we get.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
This should be interesting.

Monthly winners, or ongoing leaderboard or what?

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
Oh, this sounds awesome! EXs can still be inexplicable to some degree though, right? So that even if we can't explain exactly how, say, the possessed regenerating matchstick always burns things down, we know its harmless as long as its never touched by a bearded Italian and dropped near curtains on the summer solstice? …or something.

Either way I've had an SCP idea for a while that just didn't seem to fit, but in the context of it being EX it suddenly starts to make sense. Time to get to work!

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded: Hmm, preferably not, as they'd be main list then.

item.title: Re: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
I'm excited for this. Gonna make at least one.

Also, are there any SCPs that will be reclassified? Just a thought.

item.title: A new contest!
item.content:encoded:
Hey there kids! I'm rolling out a new SCP designation, and, with it, a contest to see who can make the best example of it.

What is the new designation? Why -EX!

An -EX, which stands for Explained, SCP is one which we have figured out. It is an SCP that is no longer an SCP, either because it has become common place, we worked out the rules behind it, or that it has just become a complete non issue.

The difference between a regular SCP and an SCP-EX are, mainly, three. First, the -EX designation. Any SCP made as an -EX needs to have a page title of SCP-###-EX. Second, the containment protocol. While I would still like to see the old containment protocols, perhaps striked out, -an EX no longer needs to be contained, for whatever reason. And, number three, at the end of the article, there needs to be a section detailing HOW it went from normal to -EX.

That said, go to it kids! I want to see what you can do with this. All examples of new -EX should be linked to SCP-EX, at least for now.

Questions?
